claim_text: We work in (1+1)-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with metric signature (+,-).
raw_response: {"claim_id": 1, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "Reducing to 1+1 dimensions is a standard and justified simplification preserving the causal structure.", "confidence": 0.95}

verdict.explanation: Reducing to 1+1 dimensions is a standard and justified simplification preserving the causal structure.

claim_text: ds^2 = dt^2 - dx^2
raw_response: {"claim_id":2,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For 1+1-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with signature (+,-), the line element is indeed ds^2 = dt^2 - dx^2 (often c=1 units).","confidence":0.99}

verdict.explanation: For 1+1-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with signature (+,-), the line element is indeed ds^2 = dt^2 - dx^2 (often c=1 units).

claim_text: An observer undergoing constant proper acceleration a has a worldline parametrized by proper time τ.

verdict.explanation: Proper time naturally parametrizes any timelike worldline, including one with constant proper acceleration.

claim_text: Assuming motion along the x-axis and that the observer is momentarily at rest at t=0 with x = 1/a.

verdict.explanation: The initial condition x=1/a at t=0 for hyperbolic motion is standard for a constant proper acceleration along the x-axis.

claim_text: From orthogonality, (dt/dτ)(d²t/dτ²) - (dx/dτ)(d²x/dτ²) = 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":8,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Differentiate normalization \(u^\mu u_\mu=(dt/d\\tau)^2-(dx/d\\tau)^2=1\): \(2[(dt/d\\tau)(d^2t/d\\tau^2)-(dx/d\\tau)(d^2x/d\\tau^2)]=0\). This is \(u\\cdot a=0\).","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: Using the constant proper acceleration condition, one finds d/dt(v/√(1-v²)) = a, where v = dx/dt.
Integrating with v=0 at t=0 gives v/√(1-v²) = at.
raw_response: {"claim_id":10,"verdict":"ERROR","explanation":"For constant proper acceleration, integrating \\(dv/(1-v^2)^{3/2}=a\,dt\\) gives \\(v/\\sqrt{1-v^2}=a t + C\\). Missing factor of \\(c\\) unless units set \\(c=1\\).","confidence":0.96}

claim_text: v = at/√(1+a²t²)
raw_response: {"claim_id": 11, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "It follows from v=tanh(aτ) with t=(1/a)sinh(aτ) and the identity tanh(arcsinh(z))=z/sqrt(1+z^2), assuming c=1.", "confidence": 1.0}

verdict.explanation: It follows from v=tanh(aτ) with t=(1/a)sinh(aτ) and the identity tanh(arcsinh(z))=z/sqrt(1+z^2), assuming c=1.

claim_text: γ = dt/dτ = √(1+a²t²)
raw_response: {"claim_id":12,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For constant proper acceleration with c=1, worldline satisfies x^2-t^2=1/a^2, giving t=(1/a)\\sinh(a\\tau), hence dt/d\\tau=\\cosh(a\\tau)=\\sqrt{1+a^2 t^2}.","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: For constant proper acceleration with c=1, worldline satisfies x^2-t^2=1/a^2, giving t=(1/a)\sinh(a\tau), hence dt/d\tau=\cosh(a\tau)=\sqrt{1+a^2 t^2}.

claim_text: Integrating dx/dt = v with x(0)=1/a yields x(t) = (1/a)√(1+a²t²).
The worldline is a hyperbola: x² - t² = 1/a².
raw_response: {"claim_id":14,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For constant proper acceleration in 1+1 Minkowski with c=1 and origin chosen appropriately, \(x(\tau)=a^{-1}\cosh a\tau,\ t(\tau)=a^{-1}\sinh a\tau\), giving \(x^2-t^2=a^{-2}\).","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: We introduce coordinates (η, ξ) that cover the right Rindler wedge x > |t|.

verdict.explanation: Standard Rindler coordinates indeed cover the right wedge defined by x > |t|.

claim_text: The coordinate transformation is t = ξ sinh η, x = ξ cosh η, with ξ > 0, -∞ < η < ∞.
raw_response: {"claim_id":16,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"Right-wedge Rindler chart only. Missing acceleration scale/c factors and wedge restriction \(x>|t|\); otherwise transformation is standard.","confidence":0.95}

claim_text: x² - t² = ξ²(cosh²η - sinh²η) = ξ².
raw_response: {"claim_id": 17, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "The derivation correctly applies the identity cosh²η − sinh²η = 1 using the standard Rindler coordinate definitions.", "confidence": 1.0}

verdict.explanation: The derivation correctly applies the identity cosh²η − sinh²η = 1 using the standard Rindler coordinate definitions.

claim_text: The observer's hyperbola x² - t² = 1/a² corresponds to ξ = 1/a = constant.
raw_response: {"claim_id":18,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"With standard Rindler map \(x=\xi\cosh\eta,\ t=\xi\sinh\eta\), constant \(\xi\) gives \(x^2-t^2=\xi^2\). Thus the hyperbola \(x^2-t^2=1/a^2\) is \(\xi=1/a\).","confidence":0.98}

claim_text: The proper time along this worldline is given by dτ = ξ dη = dη/a, so η = aτ (up to an additive constant).

verdict.explanation: For a constant Rindler coordinate ξ = 1/a, the metric gives dτ = ξ dη = dη/a, so integrating yields η = aτ up to an additive constant.

claim_text: dt = sinh η dξ + ξ cosh η dη
raw_response: {"claim_id":20,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"From standard Rindler transformation \(t=\xi\sinh\eta\), total differential gives \(dt=\sinh\eta\,d\xi+\xi\cosh\eta\,d\eta\).","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: dx = cosh η dξ + ξ sinh η dη

verdict.explanation: Differentiating x = ξ coshη by the product rule gives dx = coshη dξ + ξ sinhη dη.

claim_text: ds² = dt² - dx² = (sinh²η - cosh²η)dξ² + 2ξ(sinhη coshη - coshη sinhη)dξdη + ξ²(cosh²η - sinh²η)dη²
raw_response: {"claim_id":22,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Using \(t=\xi\\sinh\\eta, x=\xi\\cosh\\eta\), substitution gives \(ds^2=-d\\xi^2+\\xi^2 d\\eta^2\). Intermediate expanded expression is algebraically correct.","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: ds² = -dξ² + ξ²dη²
raw_response: {"claim_id": 23, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "The metric correctly transforms Minkowski coordinates to standard Rindler form with proper sign conventions.", "confidence": 1.0}

verdict.explanation: The metric correctly transforms Minkowski coordinates to standard Rindler form with proper sign conventions.

claim_text: An observer at constant ξ has proper acceleration α = 1/ξ.
raw_response: {"claim_id":24,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"For standard Rindler metric \(ds^2=\xi^2 d\eta^2-d\xi^2\) with \(c=1\), constant \(\xi\) worldlines have \(|a|=1/|\xi|\). Sign/units and coordinate convention omitted.","confidence":0.94}

claim_text: For ξ = 1/a, we recover α = a.

verdict.explanation: Standard result: constant ξ yields proper acceleration 1/ξ, so ξ=1/a implies α = a.

claim_text: The lines t = ±x (with x>0) bound the right Rindler wedge.
raw_response: {"claim_id":26,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Right Rindler wedge is \(x>|t|\), equivalently bounded by null lines \(t=\pm x\). Restricting to \(x>0\) selects the right wedge’s boundaries.","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: In Rindler coordinates, these correspond to ξ = 0 and η → ±∞.
As ξ → 0 with finite η, we approach the origin (t,x)=(0,0), but the lines t = ±x are reached by taking η → ±∞ while ξ → 0 such that t/x = tanh η → ±1.
raw_response: {"claim_id":28,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"With standard right-wedge Rindler \(t=\xi\sinh\eta,\ x=\xi\cosh\eta\): fixed finite \(\eta\), \(\xi\to0\) gives \((0,0)\); horizons \(t=\pm x\) require \(\eta\to\pm\infty\) as \(\xi\to0\).","confidence":0.98}

claim_text: The metric component g_{ηη} = ξ² vanishes at ξ=0, making it a coordinate singularity.
raw_response: {"claim_id": 29, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "g_{ηη}=ξ² indeed vanishes at ξ=0, marking the Rindler horizon as a coordinate, not physical, singularity.", "confidence": 1.0}

verdict.explanation: g_{ηη}=ξ² indeed vanishes at ξ=0, marking the Rindler horizon as a coordinate, not physical, singularity.

claim_text: In Minkowski coordinates, along t = ±x, we have ds² = 0, so these are null surfaces.
raw_response: {"claim_id":30,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For Minkowski metric \(ds^2=c^2dt^2-dx^2-dy^2-dz^2\), surfaces \(t=\pm x/c\) have tangent vectors with \(ds^2=0\) (holding \(y,z\) constant), hence are null horizons.","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: Consider a free massless scalar field φ in (1+1)-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with field equation ∂_t²φ - ∂_x²φ = 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":32,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Correct standard assumption: a free massless scalar in 1+1 Minkowski satisfies the Klein–Gordon equation, \u2202_t^2\u03c6-\u2202_x^2\u03c6=0, with c=1 and signature (+,-).","confidence":0.99}

verdict.explanation: Correct standard assumption: a free massless scalar in 1+1 Minkowski satisfies the Klein–Gordon equation, ∂_t^2φ-∂_x^2φ=0, with c=1 and signature (+,-).

claim_text: We use units where ℏ = c = k_B = 1.

verdict.explanation: Using natural units (ℏ = c = k_B = 1) is a standard, valid simplification in relativistic quantum theories.

claim_text: The positive-frequency modes (with respect to Minkowski time t) are u_k = (1/√(4π|k|)) e^{-i|k|t + ikx}.

verdict.explanation: Normalization and time dependence are standard for the massless scalar field’s positive-frequency modes in 1+1D Minkowski spacetime.

claim_text: The Minkowski vacuum |0_M⟩ is defined by a_k |0_M⟩ = 0 for all k.
raw_response: {"claim_id":38,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Standard QFT definition: Minkowski vacuum is annihilated by all positive-frequency Minkowski annihilation operators \(a_k\), for every mode label \(k\).","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: In the right Rindler wedge, we use coordinates (η, ξ) with metric ds² = ξ²dη² - dξ².

verdict.explanation: For 1+1 dimensions, using t = ξ sinh(η) and x = ξ cosh(η) yields ds² = ξ²dη² - dξ², which is correct.

claim_text: Define light-cone coordinates U = t - x, V = t + x.

verdict.explanation: The definition is standard for converting Minkowski coordinates to light-cone coordinates in (+,-) signature.

claim_text: In the right wedge, U < 0, V > 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":42,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"With Minkowski null coordinates \(U=ct-x\), \(V=ct+x\), right wedge \(x>|ct|\) implies \(V>0\) and \(U<0\).","confidence":0.99}

claim_text: The transformation to Rindler coordinates gives U = -ξ e^{-η}, V = ξ e^{η}.

verdict.explanation: It matches the standard Minkowski null coordinate transformation using t=ξ sinhη and x=ξ coshη.

claim_text: A positive-frequency Rindler mode in the right wedge is v_Ω^{(R)} = N_Ω θ(-U)(-U)^{iΩ} = N_Ω θ(-U) e^{iΩ ln(-U)}.
When expressed in (η,ξ), this becomes v_Ω^{(R)} = N_Ω e^{-iΩη} ξ^{iΩ} (up to a phase).

claim_text: For the left wedge (where U>0, V<0), we define positive-frequency modes v_Ω^{(L)} = N_Ω θ(V) V^{-iΩ}.

verdict.explanation: The definition is incorrect; for the left wedge (V<0), the mode should include θ(-V) and (-V)^{-iΩ} to ensure non-vanishing support.

claim_text: The field expansion in terms of Rindler modes is φ = ∫₀^∞ dΩ (b_Ω^{(R)} v_Ω^{(R)} + b_Ω^{(R)†} v_Ω^{(R)*} + b_Ω^{(L)} v_Ω^{(L)} + b_Ω^{(L)†} v_Ω^{(L)*}).

claim_text: Tracing over the left wedge degrees of freedom yields a thermal density matrix for the right wedge: ρ_R = ∏_Ω (1-e^{-2πΩ/a}) Σ_{n=0}^∞ e^{-2πnΩ/a} |n_Ω⟩⟨n_Ω|.
